questions.stem: A 68-year-old with progressive nocturia and incomplete emptying (smooth enlarged prostate, no nodules) is on ramipril and atenolol after an MI. Starting tamsulosin, what is the most critical counseling point given his history?
options: Combining it with his antihypertensives risks profound hypotension
High risk of acute urinary retention
It permanently lowers DHT and libido
questions.explain: α1-blockers cause vasodilation/orthostatic hypotension; added to an ACE inhibitor + beta-blocker this risks profound additive hypotension — separate dosing and counsel. (Libido/DHT effects are 5-ARIs, not α-blockers.)

questions.stem: A 34-year-old with prior corrected cryptorchidism has a firm, painless, slowly enlarging intrinsic left testicular mass. Most appropriate next step?
options: Scrotal ultrasound + serum AFP and β-hCG
questions.explain: A solid painless intrinsic testicular mass (cryptorchidism is a risk factor) needs a scrotal ultrasound + tumour markers (AFP, β-hCG). A trans-scrotal biopsy is contraindicated (seeding risk); definitive management is radical inguinal orchiectomy.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old man of African descent has worsening LUTS, a hard irregular DRE nodule, and new constant night-time axial back pain. TRUS biopsy is done. Which histology confirms the likely diagnosis?
options: Malignant glandular proliferation graded by Gleason score
questions.explain: Age, ancestry, a hard irregular nodule, and constant non-positional axial bone pain (osteoblastic mets) point to prostate adenocarcinoma — malignant glandular proliferation graded by the Gleason score.

questions.stem: A 15-year-old has sudden severe left scrotal pain and vomiting. The testis is high-riding with a horizontal lie and the cremasteric reflex is absent. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.explain: Sudden pain + absent cremasteric reflex + horizontal high-riding testis = testicular torsion, a surgical emergency. Proceed to urgent exploration and detorsion; don't delay for imaging when the diagnosis is clear (viability falls after ~6 h).

questions.stem: A patient with acute flank pain has a 7-mm obstructing ureteric stone, a temperature of 38.9°C, and pyuria. What is the priority?
options: Increase oral fluids and rescan in 6 weeks
Potassium citrate to dissolve the stone
questions.explain: An infected, obstructed urinary system is a urological emergency — source control with urgent decompression (ureteric stent or percutaneous nephrostomy) plus antibiotics. Antibiotics alone behind an obstruction are inadequate.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old smoker has a single episode of painless gross hematuria. Urine culture is negative. What is the most appropriate evaluation?
options: Cytology, CT urography, and cystoscopy to exclude urologic malignancy
questions.explain: Painless gross hematuria in an older smoker is urologic malignancy (bladder/renal) until proven otherwise → urine cytology + CT urography + cystoscopy. Smoking is the leading bladder-cancer risk factor.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman has 2 days of dysuria and frequency, now with fever, rigors, and right costovertebral-angle tenderness. What is the diagnosis and an appropriate antibiotic?
questions.explain: Fever + flank/CVA tenderness with urinary symptoms = acute pyelonephritis (upper UTI), treated with a fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin) or ceftriaxone (IV if systemically unwell). Single-dose fosfomycin is for lower cystitis only.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man with known BPH has constant dribbling; bladder scan shows 600 mL post-void residual. Which incontinence type is this, and what would be harmful?
options: Overflow incontinence; an antimuscarinic would worsen it — relieve the obstruction/retention
questions.explain: A high post-void residual with dribbling = overflow incontinence from obstruction/retention. An antimuscarinic would worsen retention; instead relieve the obstruction (α-blocker, catheter) and treat the cause. Always check PVR first.

questions.stem: A patient with classic renal colic has a normal plain KUB x-ray but ongoing symptoms. Which stone type is most likely, and what imaging is needed?
options: Uric acid stone (radiolucent); non-contrast CT or ultrasound
questions.explain: Uric acid stones are radiolucent and invisible on plain x-ray — use non-contrast CT (most sensitive) or ultrasound. Calcium and struvite stones are radiopaque.

questions.stem: A man with BPH on finasteride for a year has a PSA of 2.0 ng/mL. How should this be interpreted?
options: It is reassuringly normal as reported
Finasteride roughly halves PSA — effectively ~4.0; interpret accordingly / investigate
questions.explain: 5-α reductase inhibitors lower PSA by ~50%, so the measured value should be doubled to compare with reference ranges. A 'normal' 2.0 on finasteride corresponds to ~4.0 and may warrant evaluation.

questions.stem: Which symptom argues AGAINST simple BPH and should prompt a search for another cause?
questions.explain: Flank or back pain is not a feature of simple lower-urinary-tract disease; it suggests an alternative (stone, pyelonephritis, malignancy/metastasis). Frequency, nocturia, weak stream, and hesitancy are typical LUTS.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman leaks small amounts of urine when she coughs, laughs, or lifts, with no urgency. Which type is this and what is first-line?
questions.explain: Leakage with increased intra-abdominal pressure (cough/laugh/lift) and no urgency is stress incontinence (sphincter/pelvic-floor weakness). First-line is pelvic-floor exercises, with duloxetine or a sling as options.

questions.stem: A healthy non-pregnant woman has dysuria, frequency, and suprapubic discomfort without fever or flank pain. Which is an appropriate first-line treatment for uncomplicated cystitis?
questions.explain: Uncomplicated cystitis is treated with nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX (resistance-dependent), or single-dose fosfomycin. Fluoroquinolones are reserved (resistance/adverse effects); IV therapy is for pyelonephritis/sepsis.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old has microscopic hematuria with dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts, 1 g/day proteinuria, and hypertension. Where should the workup be directed?
questions.explain: RBC casts and dysmorphic RBCs with proteinuria/hypertension indicate a glomerular (nephritic) source → nephrology workup (serologies, possible biopsy), not a urologic cancer evaluation.

questions.stem: A bedside renal ultrasound in a patient with anuria shows a dilated, anechoic, fluid-filled renal pelvis bilaterally. What does this indicate?
questions.explain: A dilated anechoic renal pelvis = hydronephrosis, indicating obstruction (post-renal AKI) — POCUS rapidly identifies this and bladder distension, directing urgent decompression.

questions.stem: A man with metastatic prostate cancer is started on leuprolide. Why is a short course of an antiandrogen (e.g. bicalutamide) given at initiation?
options: To cover the initial testosterone 'flare' from the GnRH agonist
questions.explain: A GnRH agonist initially surges LH/testosterone (a 'flare') before desensitizing the pituitary; an antiandrogen covers this flare to prevent tumour stimulation/symptom worsening, then long-term suppression follows.

questions.stem: A patient with recurrent calcium-oxalate stones and high urinary calcium asks about prevention. Besides high fluid intake, which medication helps?
questions.explain: Thiazides reduce urinary calcium excretion and recurrent calcium-stone formation. (Potassium citrate alkalinizes urine for uric-acid and some calcium stones; loops increase calcium excretion and would be counterproductive.)

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man has a new-onset RIGHT-sided varicocele that does not decompress when he lies down. What does this warrant?
options: Imaging for a retroperitoneal mass (e.g. compressing the right gonadal/renal vein)
questions.explain: Varicoceles are usually left-sided and decompress when supine. A new right-sided or non-decompressing varicocele is a red flag for a retroperitoneal mass obstructing venous drainage → imaging.

questions.stem: On bladder POCUS the dimensions are width 8 cm, depth 6 cm, height 7 cm. Approximately what is the bladder volume?
questions.explain: Bladder volume ≈ width × depth × height × 0.7 = 8 × 6 × 7 × 0.7 ≈ 235 mL. This estimates post-void residual / retention at the bedside.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman has sudden strong urges to void followed by leakage, several times daily, with a normal post-void residual and negative urinalysis. First-line management?
options: Bladder training, then an antimuscarinic (oxybutynin) or mirabegron
questions.explain: Urgency-driven leakage with a normal PVR = urge incontinence / overactive bladder. Start with bladder training, then an antimuscarinic (oxybutynin) or mirabegron (β3-agonist, useful if antimuscarinics are contraindicated).

cards.prompt: Which prostate zone enlarges in BPH, and what symptom argues against simple BPH?
cards.answer: The transition zone (cancer favours the peripheral zone). Flank/back pain is NOT a feature of simple lower-tract disease and should prompt another diagnosis.

cards.prompt: Compare the two BPH drug classes.
cards.answer: α1-blockers (tamsulosin) relax smooth muscle for rapid relief (orthostatic hypotension, retrograde ejaculation). 5-α reductase inhibitors (finasteride) shrink the gland over ~6 months and lower PSA ~50%.

cards.prompt: Where does prostate cancer arise, and why is PSA non-specific?
cards.answer: Usually the peripheral zone. PSA rises with cancer but also BPH, prostatitis, recent ejaculation, vigorous DRE, and age — so it's sensitive but not specific (and a 5-ARI halves it).

cards.prompt: What does the Gleason score represent?
cards.answer: The sum of the most common and second-most-common histologic patterns — it grades differentiation, distinct from TNM staging (spread).

cards.prompt: How does metastatic prostate cancer classically present?
cards.answer: Deep, constant, atraumatic axial bone pain (osteoblastic metastases), weight loss, and lower-extremity edema from pelvic nodal disease.

cards.prompt: What are the signs of testicular torsion and the management?
cards.answer: Sudden severe scrotal pain, high-riding horizontal testis, thickened cord, absent cremasteric reflex → urgent surgical exploration and detorsion (don't delay for imaging if clear).

cards.prompt: How is a suspected testicular tumour worked up, and what is contraindicated?
cards.answer: Scrotal ultrasound + tumour markers (AFP, β-hCG, LDH); a trans-scrotal biopsy is contraindicated (seeding) — treat with radical inguinal orchiectomy.

cards.prompt: Which kidney stone is radiolucent, and how is it imaged?
cards.answer: Uric acid stones are radiolucent (invisible on plain x-ray); use non-contrast CT (most sensitive) or ultrasound. Calcium and struvite stones are radiopaque.

cards.prompt: When does nephrolithiasis require urgent admission/urology?
cards.answer: Obstructing stone with fever/infection (emergency decompression), bilateral obstruction or a solitary kidney, AKI, or intractable pain/vomiting preventing oral intake.

cards.prompt: Outline acute and preventive stone management.
cards.answer: Acute: NSAIDs (ketorolac) for pain, antiemetics, hydration, tamsulosin (medical expulsive therapy), urology for large/obstructing stones. Prevent: fluids, thiazide for calcium stones, potassium citrate for uric-acid/calcium stones.

cards.prompt: What does painless gross hematuria in an older smoker signify?
cards.answer: Genitourinary malignancy (bladder urothelial or renal cell carcinoma) until proven otherwise — work up with cytology, CT urography, and cystoscopy. Smoking is the leading bladder-cancer risk factor.

cards.prompt: How do you tell glomerular from urologic hematuria?
cards.answer: Glomerular: dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts, proteinuria, hypertension → nephrology. Urologic: clots, normal RBCs, no casts → image the tract and cystoscopy.

cards.prompt: Distinguish cystitis from pyelonephritis.
cards.answer: Cystitis (lower): dysuria, frequency, suprapubic pain, no fever. Pyelonephritis (upper): fever, flank/CVA tenderness, nausea — may need IV therapy/admission. E. coli is the commonest organism.

cards.prompt: Give first-line antibiotics for cystitis vs pyelonephritis.
cards.answer: Cystitis: nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX (resistance-dependent), or single-dose fosfomycin. Pyelonephritis: ciprofloxacin or ceftriaxone (IV if unwell). An infected obstructed stone needs decompression, not antibiotics alone.

cards.prompt: What are sonographic signs of an obstructing stone?
cards.answer: A hyperechoic stone with posterior acoustic shadowing, a twinkling artifact on Doppler, hydronephrosis (dilated renal pelvis), and absent ureteral jets on the affected side.

cards.prompt: Match the incontinence types to first-line treatment.
cards.answer: Stress → pelvic-floor exercises ± duloxetine; urge (OAB) → bladder training + antimuscarinic/mirabegron; overflow → relieve obstruction/retention; functional → environmental measures.

cards.prompt: BPH primarily affects which prostate zone?
Prostate cancer most commonly arises in the:
Which exam finding supports testicular torsion?
Tumour markers for testicular cancer include:
Which stone is radiolucent on plain x-ray?
Medical expulsive therapy for a distal ureteric stone uses:
A single-dose option for uncomplicated cystitis is:
Appropriate therapy for acute pyelonephritis is:
Constant dribbling with a high post-void residual indicates:
The biggest risk factor for bladder cancer is:
Select STORAGE (irritative) LUTS:
Select indications for urgent admission with a kidney stone:
Select features that make a UTI 'complicated':
Select components of a gross-hematuria workup (malignancy risk):
Select sonographic signs suggesting an obstructing stone:

cards.text: A 5-α reductase inhibitor lowers PSA by about {{50}}%, so the measured value should be {{doubled}} to interpret.
cards.explain: Important to avoid missing cancer in men on finasteride.

cards.text: Torsion: sudden pain, a {{high-riding}}/horizontal testis, and an {{absent}} cremasteric reflex → urgent {{exploration}}.
Microscopic hematuria is defined as > {{2}} RBCs per high-power field.
cards.explain: Confirm it is not menstrual or exercise-related.
Used to estimate post-void residual / retention.

cards.text: The most common organism causing UTIs is {{E. coli}}; {{nitrites}} on dipstick suggest Enterobacteriaceae.

cards.prompt: Order the workup of painless gross hematuria:
Order acute renal-colic management:
items: Non-contrast CT to confirm/size the stone
Tamsulosin (medical expulsive therapy) for small distal stones

cards.prompt: Order the approach to urinary incontinence:
items: Targeted drug or procedure by type

cards.prompt: Match the incontinence type to first-line therapy:
Match the scrotal condition to its hallmark:
Match the drug to its urologic use:
Match the infection to an appropriate antibiotic:
What two simple tests are essential when working up BPH/LUTS?
cards.answer: A post-void residual (bladder scan/uroflowmetry) to assess retention, and a urinalysis to exclude a confounding UTI or hematuria (plus a DRE).

cards.prompt: What causes struvite stones and what shape do they form?
cards.answer: Urease-producing organisms (e.g. Proteus) alkalinize the urine, forming magnesium-ammonium-phosphate stones that can grow into large 'staghorn' calculi.

cards.prompt: When should asymptomatic bacteriuria be treated?
cards.answer: In pregnancy (and before urologic procedures) — otherwise asymptomatic bacteriuria is generally not treated, to limit resistance.

cards.prompt: Why do α1-blockers cause retrograde ejaculation?
cards.answer: α1A receptors normally close the bladder neck during ejaculation; blocking them lets semen flow backward into the bladder (appearing later on urinalysis).

cards.prompt: What does chronic kidney disease look like on ultrasound?
cards.answer: Small, echogenic kidneys with thinned cortices — helps distinguish chronic disease from acute (where kidneys are usually normal-sized).

cards.prompt: What local symptoms can locally advanced prostate cancer cause?
cards.answer: Obstructive LUTS, erectile dysfunction (cavernous-nerve invasion), and gross hematuria or hematospermia.

cards.prompt: How do stress and urge incontinence differ clinically?
cards.answer: Stress: small leaks with cough/laugh/exertion (sphincter weakness), no urgency. Urge: sudden compelling urgency then leakage (detrusor overactivity). They guide different first-line therapy.

guides.title: Week 35 · 1 Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia
guides.html:
BPH

Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia

Week 35 · KU III — Urology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The transition-zone enlargement, the LUTS categories, the workup that rules out mimics, and the two complementary drug classes.

Anatomy & presentation

BPH is hyperplasia of the prostate's transition zone (cancer favours the peripheral zone). It causes lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS):

CategorySymptoms
Storage (irritative)Frequency, urgency, nocturia, urgency incontinence
Voiding (obstructive)Hesitancy, weak stream, intermittency, straining
Post-micturitionDribbling, incomplete emptying

Flank/back pain is NOT a feature of simple lower-tract disease — look elsewhere (stone, malignancy, pyelonephritis).

Workup

  • Post-void residual (bladder scan/uroflowmetry) to assess retention; urinalysis must be clear of leukocyte esterase/nitrites to exclude a confounding UTI. DRE: smooth, rubbery, symmetrically enlarged (a hard nodule suggests cancer).

Pharmacotherapy — two complementary classes

ClassEffectCaution
α1-blocker (tamsulosin, alfuzosin)Rapid relief — relaxes prostate/bladder-neck smooth muscleOrthostatic hypotension (additive with antihypertensives), retrograde ("dry") ejaculation
5-α reductase inhibitor (finasteride, dutasteride)Shrinks the gland over ~6 months (blocks testosterone→DHT)↓Libido, impotence, gynecomastia; lowers PSA ~50%; teratogen
BPH = transition-zone LUTS (storage/voiding/post-micturition); flank pain is not BPH. Exclude UTI and check PVR. α1-blocker for fast relief (orthostatic hypotension, retrograde ejaculation) + 5-ARI to shrink the gland (halves PSA) — often combined.

guides.title: Week 35 · 2 Prostate Cancer
guides.html:
Malignancy

Prostate Cancer

Week 35 · KU III — Urology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Peripheral-zone adenocarcinoma, the PSA caveats, Gleason grading, the metastatic red flags, and androgen-deprivation therapy.

Where it is and how it presents

Prostate adenocarcinoma usually arises in the peripheral zone. Early disease is asymptomatic; locally advanced disease causes obstruction, ED (nerve invasion), or hematuria/hematospermia.

Metastatic red flags
Unexplained weight loss, night sweats, lower-extremity edema (pelvic nodes), and deep, atraumatic axial bone pain (osteoblastic bone metastases) — classically constant and worse at night. A hard, irregular, asymmetric nodule on DRE is suspicious.

PSA — sensitive, not specific

PSA liquefies semen; it rises with cancer but also with BPH, prostatitis, recent ejaculation, vigorous DRE, cycling, and age. Use it for shared-decision screening, not as a standalone diagnosis. (A 5-ARI roughly halves PSA — double the value to interpret.)

Diagnosis & grading

  • TRUS-guided biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma (indicated by a suspicious DRE nodule or rising PSA).
  • Gleason score = sum of the most common + second-most-common histologic patterns (grades differentiation). Don't confuse with TNM staging (spread).

Advanced therapy — total androgen blockade

  • Testicular suppression: orchiectomy, or medical castration with a GnRH agonist (leuprolide) — continuous dosing desensitizes the pituitary and shuts down the gonads (a transient initial "flare" is covered by an antiandrogen).
  • Adrenal-androgen blockade: a non-steroidal antiandrogen (bicalutamide, enzalutamide).
Peripheral-zone adenocarcinoma; PSA is sensitive but non-specific. Gleason grades differentiation (≠ TNM stage). Deep axial bone pain = osteoblastic mets. Advanced disease → androgen deprivation (leuprolide + antiandrogen) or orchiectomy.

guides.title: Week 35 · 3 Testicular Conditions
guides.html:
Scrotum

Testicular Conditions

Week 35 · KU III — Urology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The torsion emergency, the painless-mass tumour workup (and the biopsy you must not do), and varicocele.

Testicular torsion — a surgical emergency

Time-critical
Sudden, severe, unilateral scrotal pain ± nausea (often during exertion or sleep). Exam: high-riding, horizontal-lie testis, thickened cord, and an absent cremasteric reflex. This is a urological emergency — urgent surgical exploration + detorsion (don't delay for imaging if the diagnosis is clear); viability falls sharply after ~6 hours.

Testicular malignancy

  • Firm, painless, intrinsic testicular nodule that slowly enlarges in a young man; cryptorchidism (even after correction) is a risk factor.
  • Workup: scrotal ultrasound + tumour markers AFP and β-hCG (and LDH).
  • Do NOT do a trans-scrotal biopsy — it risks seeding tumour into scrotal lymphatics; diagnosis/treatment is radical inguinal orchiectomy.

Varicocele

  • Dilated pampiniform-plexus veins ("bag of worms"), usually left-sided; classically decompresses when supine.
  • A new right-sided or non-decompressing varicocele → image for a retroperitoneal mass. Treat surgically only for testicular atrophy or impaired fertility.
Sudden pain + absent cremasteric reflex + horizontal lie = torsion → OR now. Painless firm intrinsic mass = tumour → ultrasound + AFP/β-hCG, NO scrotal biopsy, inguinal orchiectomy. Left varicocele common; new right-sided one → look for a mass.

guides.title: Week 35 · 4 Nephrolithiasis (Kidney Stones)
guides.html:
Stones

Nephrolithiasis (Kidney Stones)

Week 35 · KU III — Urology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Stone types and what's radiolucent, the colicky presentation, the must-admit criteria, and acute vs preventive management.

Presentation

Acute, severe, colicky flank pain radiating to the groin, often with hematuria, nausea, and an inability to sit still. Most pass spontaneously if small (<5 mm).

Stone types & imaging

StoneNote
Calcium oxalate (most common), calcium phosphate, struviteRadiopaque on plain x-ray
Uric acidRadiolucent (invisible on KUB) — need non-contrast CT or ultrasound
Struvite (Mg-ammonium-phosphate)Urease-producing organisms (Proteus); "staghorn" calculi

Non-contrast CT is the most sensitive test; ultrasound (and a low-dose CT) limits radiation.

Admit / urgent urology when
Obstructing stone + fever/infection (infected obstructed system = emergency — decompress + antibiotics), bilateral obstruction or obstruction of a solitary kidney (post-renal failure risk), AKI, or intractable vomiting/pain (can't tolerate oral intake).

Management

  • Acute: analgesia (NSAIDs/ketorolac first-line, opioids if needed), antiemetics, hydration; medical expulsive therapy with an α-blocker (tamsulosin) for distal ureteric stones; urology (lithotripsy/ureteroscopy) for large/obstructing stones.
  • Prevention: high fluid intake; for calcium stones, a thiazide (lowers urine calcium) and dietary changes; potassium citrate for uric-acid/calcium stones (alkalinizes urine).
Colicky flank-to-groin pain; uric-acid stones are radiolucent (need CT/US). Infected + obstructed stone = emergency decompression. NSAIDs for pain, tamsulosin to expel, thiazide/citrate to prevent.

guides.title: Week 35 · 5 Hematuria & Bladder Cancer
guides.html:
Blood in urine

Hematuria & Bladder Cancer

Week 35 · KU III — Urology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The micro-hematuria definition, the gross-hematuria split (UTI vs malignancy), and the workup that must not miss cancer.

Definitions

Microscopic hematuria = >2 RBCs/high-power field on a properly collected sample (confirm it's not menstrual/exercise-related). Gross hematuria is visible blood.

Painless gross hematuria = cancer until proven otherwise
In an older adult or smoker, painless gross hematuria carries a high risk of genitourinary malignancy (bladder urothelial carcinoma, renal cell carcinoma) and mandates full evaluation. In a peri-menopausal woman, the commonest cause of gross hematuria is actually a UTI — but malignancy must still be excluded.

Glomerular vs urologic bleeding

  • Glomerular (nephritic): RBC casts, dysmorphic RBCs, proteinuria, hypertension → nephrology.
  • Urologic: clots, normal-shaped RBCs, no casts → image the tract and scope the bladder.

Workup

  • Urinalysis + microscopy, urine culture (exclude infection) and cytology; CT urography (or renal/bladder ultrasound) to image the upper tracts; cystoscopy to inspect the bladder.
  • Bladder cancer risk factors: smoking (the biggest), occupational aromatic amines, chronic irritation; most are urothelial carcinoma presenting with painless hematuria.
Micro-hematuria = >2 RBC/HPF. Painless gross hematuria in an older adult/smoker = urologic malignancy until excluded (cytology + CT urogram + cystoscopy). RBC casts redirect you to a glomerular (nephritic) cause.

guides.title: Week 35 · 6 Urinary Tract Infection
guides.html:
Infection

Urinary Tract Infection

Week 35 · KU III — Urology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Cystitis vs pyelonephritis, the complicated-vs-uncomplicated split, and first-line antibiotics (with what to avoid).

The spectrum

Cystitis (lower)Pyelonephritis (upper)
SymptomsDysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain; no feverFever, flank pain, CVA tenderness, nausea ± cystitis symptoms
SettingOften outpatientMay need admission/IV therapy

Most common organism: E. coli. Diagnose with urinalysis (leukocyte esterase, nitrites) and culture; nitrites suggest Enterobacteriaceae.

Uncomplicated vs complicated

  • Uncomplicated: healthy non-pregnant women, normal tract.
  • Complicated: men, pregnancy, catheters, obstruction/stones, immunocompromise, or structural/functional abnormality — broader coverage, longer course, and imaging if obstruction suspected.
First-line antibiotics
Uncomplicated cystitis: nitrofurantoin, TMP-SMX (resistance-dependent), or fosfomycin (single dose). Pyelonephritis: a fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin) or ceftriaxone (IV if unwell). Pregnancy: treat asymptomatic bacteriuria; avoid fluoroquinolones; nitrofurantoin not at term. An infected obstructed stone needs urgent decompression, not antibiotics alone.
Cystitis = lower (dysuria, no fever); pyelonephritis = fever + flank/CVA tenderness. E. coli predominates. Nitrofurantoin/TMP-SMX/fosfomycin for cystitis; cipro/ceftriaxone for pyelonephritis. Obstruction + infection = drain it.

guides.title: Week 35 · 7 Renal & Bladder Point-of-Care Ultrasound
guides.html:
POCUS

Renal & Bladder Point-of-Care Ultrasound

Week 35 · KU III — Urology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

How POCUS rapidly answers the urgent urology questions — hydronephrosis, stones, and bladder volume.

Why POCUS

A rapid, radiation-free bedside tool to assess the renal parenchyma and bladder — especially to detect hydronephrosis (the key finding in obstruction) and urinary retention.

Renal scan

  • Image both kidneys in long and short axis (right kidney via the liver, left via the spleen, using the diaphragm as a landmark); scan Morison's pouch (hepatorenal space) for free fluid.
  • Assess the capsule, cortex, medullary pyramids, and renal pelvis; chronic CKD shows small, echogenic kidneys with thinned cortices.
Sonographic signs of stones / obstruction
A hyperechoic stone with posterior acoustic shadowing, a twinkling artifact on color Doppler, hydronephrosis (dilated anechoic renal pelvis), and absent ureteral jets into the bladder on the affected side.

Bladder scan

  • Image in transverse and longitudinal planes. Bladder volume = width × depth × height × 0.7 (used for post-void residual / retention).
  • Windows differ by sex: male — bladder, seminal vesicles, recto-vesical space; female — bladder, uterus, vesico-uterine and recto-uterine (Pouch of Douglas) spaces.
POCUS answers "is there obstruction (hydronephrosis) or retention (bladder volume)?" fast and without radiation. Stones: hyperechoic + shadowing + twinkling + absent ureteral jet. Bladder volume = W×D×H×0.7.

guides.title: Week 35 · 8 LUTS Approach & Urinary Incontinence
guides.html:
Approach

LUTS Approach & Urinary Incontinence

Week 35 · KU III — Urology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

A structured read of lower urinary tract symptoms, and the incontinence types with their targeted treatments.

Reading LUTS

Sort symptoms into storage (frequency, urgency, nocturia), voiding (hesitancy, weak stream, intermittency), and post-micturition (dribbling, incomplete emptying). Always check a urinalysis (exclude UTI/hematuria) and a post-void residual, and do a DRE in men.

Urinary incontinence types

TypeMechanism / clueFirst-line
StressLeak with cough/laugh/exertion; weak sphincter/pelvic floor (post-partum, post-prostatectomy)Pelvic-floor (Kegel) exercises; duloxetine; surgery (sling)
Urge (overactive bladder)Sudden urgency → leak; detrusor overactivityBladder training; antimuscarinic (oxybutynin) or mirabegron
OverflowDribbling from a chronically full bladder (BPH obstruction, atonic/neurogenic bladder, diabetes)Relieve obstruction (α-blocker, catheter); treat retention
FunctionalIntact tract but can't reach the toilet (mobility, cognition)Environmental/toileting measures
Don't miss overflow
An elderly man with "incontinence" and a palpable/scanned full bladder has overflow from obstruction or retention — an antimuscarinic would worsen it. Check the post-void residual first.
Stress = leak with effort (Kegels/duloxetine); urge = OAB (antimuscarinic/mirabegron); overflow = retention/obstruction (relieve it, check PVR); functional = access problem. Always exclude UTI and measure PVR before treating.

guides.title: Week 35 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

KU III — Urology

Week 35 · KU III — Urology
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Testicular torsion (sudden pain, absent cremasteric reflex, horizontal lie) → urgent surgical exploration — don't wait for imaging
Infected + obstructed stone (fever + obstruction) → emergency decompression (stent/nephrostomy) + antibiotics
Painless gross hematuria in an older adult/smoker → urologic malignancy until excluded (cytology + CT urogram + cystoscopy)
Don't biopsy a testicular mass trans-scrotally → seeding; do US + AFP/β-hCG, then inguinal orchiectomy
Overflow incontinence (high PVR) → an antimuscarinic worsens it; relieve the obstruction/retention
New right-sided / non-decompressing varicocele → image for a retroperitoneal mass

BPH BPH

Zone transition zone (cancer = peripheral)
LUTS storage / voiding / post-micturition
Not BPH flank/back pain
Workup PVR + urinalysis (exclude UTI), DRE
α1-blocker rapid relief; orthostatic hypotension, retrograde ejaculation
5-ARI shrinks gland ~6 mo; halves PSA

Prostate cancer Prostate Ca

Zone peripheral
PSA sensitive, not specific
Gleason grades differentiation (≠ TNM stage)
Mets osteoblastic axial bone pain
Diagnosis TRUS biopsy
Advanced Rx GnRH agonist (+antiandrogen for flare) / orchiectomy

Testicular Testis

Torsion absent cremasteric reflex → OR now
Tumour painless firm mass; US + AFP/β-hCG
Biopsy trans-scrotal contraindicated (seeding)
Risk factor cryptorchidism
Varicocele left common; new right → image

Stones Stones

Pain colicky flank → groin
Radiolucent uric acid (need CT/US)
Struvite urease (Proteus); staghorn
Emergency infected + obstructed → decompress
Acute Rx NSAID + tamsulosin (MET)
Prevent fluids; thiazide (Ca); citrate (uric acid)

Hematuria / UTI Hematuria/UTI

Micro-hematuria >2 RBC/HPF
Painless gross older/smoker → malignancy workup
RBC casts glomerular (nephritic) → nephrology
Cystitis nitrofurantoin / TMP-SMX / fosfomycin
Pyelonephritis cipro / ceftriaxone; fever + flank
Pregnancy treat asymptomatic bacteriuria

POCUS / incontinence POCUS/UI

Hydronephrosis = obstruction (post-renal)
Stone signs shadowing, twinkling, absent jet
Bladder volume W×D×H×0.7
Stress leak with effort → Kegels/duloxetine
Urge OAB → antimuscarinic/mirabegron
Overflow high PVR → relieve obstruction
Week 35 · KU III — Urology · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.mechanism: α1-blocker — relaxes prostate/bladder-neck (and ureteric) smooth muscle
GnRH agonist — continuous dosing desensitizes the pituitary → ↓testosterone
facets.adverse: Initial flare (cover with antiandrogen), hot flashes, bone loss

facets.mechanism: Non-steroidal antiandrogen — blocks the androgen receptor
Alkalinizes urine; raises urinary citrate (inhibits crystallization)
Concentrates in urine, damages bacterial DNA
facets.adverse: Pulmonary toxicity (chronic); avoid if low eGFR or at term/pyelonephritis
